suggestions.name: SectionOutlinePaintRegistry
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive paint management system that maintains mappings between section keys and outline paint objects. Extracting them into a registry improves separation of concerns by isolating paint storage and retrieval logic from the main plotting functionality.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage the outline paint properties for pie chart sections. They handle storage, retrieval, modification, and automatic population of paint objects that define how section outlines are drawn. The main responsibility is maintaining a mapping between section keys and their corresponding outline paint configurations.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "section outline paint management" domain. They represent a cohesive subsystem for handling paint attributes specifically for pie chart section outlines, including default values, per-section customization, and automatic paint generation from drawing suppliers.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates outline paint management from other pie chart concerns
- Code reusability: Paint mapping logic could be reused for other chart elements (fills, strokes)
- Complexity reduction: Removes paint management complexity from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Paint mapping behavior can be tested independently

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate the concern of managing paint mappings. It represents a specialized Registry pattern for paint objects, providing both storage and intelligent retrieval with fallback mechanisms.

suggestions.name: SectionOutlineStrokeRegistry
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive registry system that manages the mapping between section keys and their outline stroke properties. Extracting them creates a specialized registry that encapsulates stroke lookup, storage, and default value management, separating this concern from the main plotting logic.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage section outline strokes for pie chart sections. They provide a complete CRUD interface for storing, retrieving, updating, and clearing stroke configurations mapped to section keys, along with default stroke management and automatic population capabilities.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "section outline stroke mapping" domain. They represent a specialized key-value storage system that maps section identifiers to their corresponding outline stroke properties, with fallback to defaults and auto-population from drawing suppliers.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates stroke mapping logic from the main plotting concerns
- Code reusability: This mapping pattern could be reused for other visual properties or chart types
- Complexity reduction: Removes stroke management complexity from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Stroke mapping logic can be tested independently of plotting functionality

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate data management concerns. This creates a specialized registry that encapsulates the mapping between keys and stroke objects, following the Registry pattern for managing collections of related objects with lookup capabilities.

suggestions.name: PieChartConfiguration
suggestions.rationale: These members collectively represent the complete configuration state of a pie chart, including visual styling, layout properties, labeling settings, and behavioral options. Extracting them into a configuration object improves encapsulation by grouping all related settings together and reduces the complexity of the main PiePlot class by separating configuration management from rendering logic.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
The main responsibility shared by these methods is managing the complete configuration state of a pie chart plot. The getLabelLinkStyle() and setLabelLinkStyle() methods handle one specific aspect of this configuration, while the hashCode() method computes a hash based on ALL configuration fields. Together, they represent the complete state management of pie chart visual properties and behavior settings.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "configuration" or "settings" domain of a pie chart. The extensive list of fields covers all aspects of pie chart appearance and behavior: visual styling (colors, fonts, strokes), layout properties (angles, gaps, positioning), labeling configuration, shadow effects, and interaction settings. This is a cohesive configuration object that encapsulates all customizable aspects of pie chart rendering.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Separates configuration management from chart rendering logic
- Code reusability: Configuration can be shared, copied, or applied to multiple pie charts
- Complexity reduction: Removes 40+ configuration fields from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Configuration logic can be tested independently from rendering logic
- Maintainability: All related configuration properties are grouped together

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to create a Configuration Object or Parameter Object. This design pattern is commonly used to group related parameters and settings into a cohesive unit, reducing parameter lists and improving encapsulation of configuration concerns.

suggestions.name: PieLabelLinkRenderer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive rendering unit responsible for drawing the visual connections between pie chart labels and their corresponding slices. They share styling properties and complex geometric calculations for different link styles, making them ideal candidates for extraction into a specialized renderer class that focuses solely on label link visualization.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility: rendering the visual links that connect pie chart labels to their corresponding pie slices. The fields control the appearance (visibility, paint, stroke) of these links, while the drawing methods handle the actual geometric calculations and rendering of different link styles (standard lines, quad curves, cubic curves) for both left and right positioned labels.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "label link rendering" domain within pie chart visualization. They encapsulate the complete lifecycle of label link display - from configuration (visibility, styling) to actual drawing operations. The drawing methods contain complex geometric calculations for positioning links between pie slices and labels, while the accessor methods manage the visual properties.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates label link rendering logic from the broader pie plot concerns
- Enhances Reusability: Link rendering logic could be reused by other chart types that need similar label connections
- Reduces Complexity: Removes intricate geometric calculations and rendering code from the main PiePlot class
- Improves Testability: Label link rendering can be tested independently with mock graphics contexts

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate rendering concerns. This creates a specialized renderer component that follows the Strategy pattern for different link styles, improving cohesion by grouping related rendering operations while reducing coupling with the main plot class.

suggestions.name: PieLabelRenderer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive label rendering unit responsible for drawing pie chart labels with shadows and styling. They share rendering state, coordinate the complete label drawing process, and encapsulate all visual aspects of label presentation. Extracting them creates a focused renderer that can handle label visualization independently from the main plot logic.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively handle the visual rendering and styling of pie chart labels, specifically focusing on simple label rendering with shadow effects. The methods manage shadow positioning (shadowXOffset, shadowYOffset), label positioning (simpleLabelOffset), shadow generation, and the complete rendering pipeline for simple labels including background, outline, shadow, and text drawing.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "label rendering" domain within pie chart visualization. They represent a cohesive rendering subsystem that handles the visual presentation of labels, including their positioning, styling (fonts, colors, backgrounds), and visual effects (shadows). The drawSimpleLabels method orchestrates the entire rendering process using the configuration provided by the other methods.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates label rendering concerns from the main plot logic
- Code reusability: Label rendering logic can be reused across different chart types or contexts
- Complexity reduction: Removes complex rendering logic from the main PiePlot class, making it more focused on plot coordination
- Testability: Label rendering can be tested independently with mock graphics contexts

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate rendering concerns. It implements the Strategy pattern concept where label rendering becomes a pluggable component. The extraction creates high cohesion (all methods work together to render labels) and low coupling (minimal dependencies on the parent plot class).

suggestions.name: PieSectionRenderer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for rendering individual pie chart sections, including drawing the slices, applying visual effects like shadows and outlines, and creating interactive entities. Extracting them into a PieSectionRenderer separates the complex rendering logic from the main plot coordination, improving maintainability and allowing for different rendering strategies.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively handle the visual rendering of individual pie chart sections. The main responsibility is drawing pie slices with their associated visual properties (shadows, outlines, fills) and creating interactive entities with tooltips and URLs. The drawItem method orchestrates the rendering process, while getArcBounds handles geometric calculations for exploded sections.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "pie section rendering" domain. They work together to transform data values into visual pie slices, handling geometric calculations, visual styling, shadow effects, and interactive features. The fields store rendering configuration (shadow properties, generators, visibility flags) while methods perform the actual drawing operations.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates section rendering logic from overall plot management
- Complexity reduction: Removes complex drawing logic from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Section rendering can be tested independently with mock graphics contexts
- Reusability: Could potentially be reused by different pie plot implementations

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate rendering concerns. The Strategy pattern could be applied here, where different section renderers could be plugged in. This extraction supports the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a focused component that only handles the visual representation of pie sections.

suggestions.name: PieLegendRenderer
suggestions.rationale: These methods and fields form a cohesive unit responsible for rendering pie chart legends, including section styling, label generation, and legend item creation. Extracting them into a PieLegendRenderer class separates legend-specific concerns from core plotting logic, improving maintainability and enabling reuse across different chart contexts.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage the visual representation and metadata generation for pie chart legends. They handle section paint management (getting, setting, clearing section paints), legend label generation (including tooltips and URLs), and the creation of legend items with proper styling and metadata. The primary responsibility is managing all aspects of how pie chart sections appear and behave in legends.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "legend rendering" domain within the pie chart visualization system. They work together to transform raw pie chart data into styled, interactive legend items with proper labels, colors, tooltips, and URL links. The fields store the configuration and styling information, while the methods use this configuration to generate complete legend representations.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Separates legend-specific concerns from general pie chart plotting logic
- Code reusability: Legend rendering logic could be reused by other chart types or legend systems
- Complexity reduction: Removes legend-specific complexity from the main PiePlot class, making it more focused on core plotting responsibilities
- Testability: Legend generation logic can be tested independently with mock data and configurations

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns based on the Single Responsibility Principle. The extracted class represents a cohesive "Legend Renderer" that encapsulates all legend-related styling, generation, and formatting logic. It demonstrates high cohesion (all methods work together to create legends) and can achieve low coupling through dependency injection of required chart data.

suggestions.name: PieLabelRenderer
suggestions.rationale: These members form a cohesive label rendering subsystem that should be extracted into a PieLabelRenderer class. They collectively handle all aspects of pie chart label visualization - from styling configuration to spatial distribution and actual drawing. Extracting them separates rendering concerns from the main plotting logic, improving maintainability and enabling reuse across different chart implementations.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively handle the rendering and positioning of labels in a pie chart. They manage label appearance properties (font, paint, padding, gap), coordinate the distribution of labels to avoid overlaps, and execute the actual drawing of labels on both left and right sides of the pie chart. The core responsibility is transforming label data into visually positioned and styled text elements.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "label rendering" domain within the pie chart visualization system. They represent a cohesive subsystem that encapsulates everything needed to render pie section labels: styling configuration, spatial distribution logic, and the rendering pipeline. The fields define the visual appearance while the methods orchestrate the label positioning and drawing process.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates label rendering concerns from the main plotting logic
- Code reusability: Label rendering logic could be reused by other chart types or pie chart variants
- Complexity reduction: Removes ~13 fields and multiple complex methods from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Label rendering behavior can be tested independently with mock graphics contexts

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate rendering concerns. The extraction creates a specialized renderer that follows the Strategy pattern, where different label rendering strategies could be plugged into the main plot. This improves cohesion by grouping all label-related functionality and reduces coupling by creating a clear interface between plotting and label rendering.

suggestions.name: PiePlotConfiguration
suggestions.rationale: These members collectively manage the complete configuration state of a pie plot, including visual styling, labeling, data handling, and rendering parameters. Extracting them into a dedicated configuration class separates concerns between plot configuration and plot rendering, improving maintainability and enabling configuration reuse across multiple pie plots.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods and fields are responsible for initializing and managing the configuration state of a PiePlot. The constructor methods set up all the default values for visual appearance, data handling, labeling, and behavioral properties. The getMinimumArcAngleToDraw() and setMinimumArcAngleToDraw() methods manage one specific configuration parameter. Together, they handle the complete initialization and configuration management of pie plot settings.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All these members belong to the "configuration" domain of a pie chart plot. They represent the complete set of customizable properties that define how a pie chart should be rendered, including colors, fonts, spacing, angles, label settings, and behavioral flags. This is essentially a configuration object that encapsulates all the settable parameters for pie plot visualization.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Separates configuration management from actual plotting/rendering logic
- Code reusability: Configuration could be shared, copied, or applied to multiple pie plots
- Complexity reduction: The main PiePlot class becomes focused on rendering rather than managing dozens of configuration fields
- Testability: Configuration logic can be tested independently from rendering logic
- Maintainability: All default values and configuration logic are centralized in one place

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to create a Configuration Object. This is a common pattern where complex objects with many configuration parameters benefit from having their configuration separated into a dedicated class. It also supports the Builder pattern for fluent configuration setup.

suggestions.name: PiePlotConfiguration
suggestions.rationale: These members collectively manage the complete configuration state of a pie chart, including visual properties, behavioral settings, and rendering options. Extracting them into a configuration class separates chart settings management from core plotting logic, improving maintainability and enabling configuration reuse across multiple pie plots.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
The main responsibility shared by these methods is managing and comparing the complete configuration state of a PiePlot. The getLabelLinkMargin() and setLabelLinkMargin() methods handle a specific configuration property, while the equals() method performs comprehensive state comparison across all configuration fields including visual properties, labels, shadows, tooltips, and behavioral settings.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "configuration" or "settings" domain of the PiePlot. They collectively manage the complete set of customizable properties that define how a pie chart should be rendered and behave. The fields encompass visual styling (paints, strokes, fonts), layout properties (gaps, angles, offsets), behavioral flags (visibility, auto-population), and functional components (generators, distributors).

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Separates configuration management from chart rendering logic
- Code reusability: Configuration could be shared, copied, or applied to multiple pie plots
- Complexity reduction: The main PiePlot class becomes focused on core plotting functionality
- Testability: Configuration logic can be tested independently, and the massive equals() method becomes more manageable

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate data management concerns from business logic. The extracted class would be a configuration object or settings container, following the Parameter Object pattern to encapsulate related configuration data and operations.

suggestions.name: PieChartStyleConfiguration
suggestions.rationale: These members form a cohesive unit responsible for managing all visual styling properties of a pie chart including paints, strokes, and shapes for sections, labels, shadows, and legends. Extracting them into a dedicated configuration class separates styling concerns from core plotting logic, improving maintainability and enabling style reuse across different chart instances.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage the visual styling and appearance properties of a pie chart. They handle getting/setting various Paint objects (for colors), Stroke objects (for line styles), and Shape objects (for visual elements), along with custom serialization logic to persist these graphical properties.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "visual styling" or "appearance configuration" domain. They represent a cohesive set of graphical properties that define how different parts of a pie chart look - sections, labels, shadows, outlines, and legend items. The serialization methods also operate exclusively on these same styling properties.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Separates visual styling concerns from chart plotting logic
- Code reusability: Styling configuration could be shared across different chart types or reused in themes
- Complexity reduction: The main PiePlot class becomes more focused on core plotting responsibilities
- Testability: Styling logic can be tested independently from chart rendering logic
- Maintainability: All appearance-related changes are centralized in one class

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns. It creates a Value Object or Configuration Object that encapsulates related data and behavior. The styling properties have high cohesion (all related to visual appearance) and can have low coupling with the main plotting logic.

suggestions.name: SectionPaintResolver
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for resolving and transforming Paint objects for pie chart sections. They handle the complex logic of paint lookup with auto-population and geometric adjustment of gradient paints, representing a distinct responsibility that can be separated from the main plotting logic.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively handle the lookup and dynamic adjustment of paint objects for pie chart sections. The main responsibility is to retrieve appropriate Paint objects for sections, with special handling for RadialGradientPaint that requires geometric adjustments based on the current pie segment's position and size.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "section paint resolution" domain within pie chart rendering. They encapsulate the logic for determining how individual pie sections should be painted, including auto-population behavior and dynamic paint transformation based on geometric properties of the pie segments.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates paint lookup and transformation logic from the broader plotting concerns
- Code reusability: Paint resolution logic could be reused by other chart types or pie plot variants
- Complexity reduction: Removes specialized paint handling from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Paint resolution logic can be tested independently with mock geometric data

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate the concern of "how sections get their paint" from the broader pie plotting responsibilities. The Strategy pattern could be applied here, where different paint resolution strategies could be plugged in.

suggestions.name: PieDataRenderer
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for managing pie chart data and translating it into visual rendering parameters like explosion offsets and shadow effects. They handle the complete lifecycle from data access to data-driven visual effect calculations. Extracting them creates a focused component that bridges raw data and visual representation.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage the data source for the pie chart and handle data-driven visual effects. The core responsibilities include: managing the dataset lifecycle (get/set with proper listener registration), accessing data sections by key, managing explode percentages for visual separation of pie sections, calculating maximum explode values, initializing plot state based on data, and rendering the pie with data-dependent visual effects like shadows and explosion offsets.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "data management and data-driven rendering" domain of pie charts. They represent the bridge between raw data (PieDataset) and visual representation, handling both data access patterns and data-dependent visual effects like section explosion and shadow generation. The fields (dataset, direction, explodePercentages, shadowGenerator) all serve this data-to-visual-effect mapping.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Separates data management and data-driven visual effects from other plotting concerns like styling, labeling, and layout
- Code reusability: This data management logic could be reused by other pie chart variants or similar data visualization components
- Complexity reduction: The main PiePlot class becomes more focused on core plotting mechanics rather than data lifecycle management
- Testability: Data management and explosion calculations can be tested independently from graphics rendering

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate data management concerns from presentation logic. It implements the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a cohesive unit that handles dataset lifecycle, data access, and data-dependent visual effects. The extraction creates high cohesion (all methods work with the same data structures) and reduces coupling in the original class.

suggestions.name: PieRenderingConfiguration
suggestions.rationale: These methods and fields form a cohesive unit responsible for configuring how pie chart sections are rendered, positioned, and labeled. Extracting them into a configuration class separates rendering concerns from data management, improving maintainability and allowing the rendering behavior to be tested and reused independently.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage the configuration and rendering behavior of pie chart sections. They control how pie slices are positioned (startAngle, direction), which data values should be included in the visualization (ignoreNullValues, ignoreZeroValues), and how labels are drawn and positioned around the pie chart. The drawLabels method orchestrates the complex process of calculating label positions and delegating to left/right label drawing methods.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "pie chart rendering configuration" domain. They form a cohesive unit that encapsulates all the settings and logic needed to determine how pie sections should be displayed, positioned, and labeled. The fields work together to define the visual presentation rules, while the methods implement the rendering logic based on these configurations.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Separates rendering configuration from other plot concerns like data management or event handling
- Code reusability: The rendering configuration could be reused across different pie plot implementations
- Complexity reduction: Removes complex label positioning logic from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Rendering behavior can be tested independently without the full plot infrastructure

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns. The rendering configuration represents a cohesive Value Object or Configuration Object that encapsulates related data and behavior. It supports the Single Responsibility Principle by isolating rendering concerns from the broader plotting responsibilities.

suggestions.name: PieLayoutCalculator
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for calculating the geometric layout of pie chart elements. They share spatial configuration parameters and work together to compute the nested areas (pie area, explode area, link area) required for proper pie chart rendering. Extracting them creates a focused component that handles the complex mathematical aspects of pie chart layout.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively handle the geometric layout and spatial calculations for rendering a pie chart. They manage interior gaps, circular constraints, label spacing, and most importantly, the complex mathematical calculations in drawPie() that determine the various rendering areas (link area, explode area, pie area) based on spacing parameters and geometric constraints.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All members belong to the "geometric layout" domain of pie chart rendering. They share spatial configuration fields (interiorGap, circular, maximumLabelWidth, labelGap, etc.) and work together to calculate the nested rectangular/circular areas needed for proper pie chart display. The drawPie() method orchestrates these calculations using the configuration parameters.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates complex geometric calculations from other pie chart concerns (data handling, styling, event handling)
- Reduces Complexity: The main PiePlot class becomes more focused on high-level plotting logic
- Testability: Geometric calculations can be unit tested independently with various spacing configurations
- Reusability: Layout logic could potentially be reused by other circular chart types

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate geometric layout concerns. The extracted class would have high cohesion (all methods deal with spatial calculations) and low coupling (minimal dependencies beyond basic geometric types). This aligns with the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a focused component for pie chart layout mathematics.

suggestions.name: SectionOutlineStrokeAutoPopulator
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for managing automatic population of section outline strokes in pie charts. They encapsulate the configuration flag and lookup logic that determines when missing stroke values should be auto-populated, representing a single well-defined responsibility that can be extracted to reduce the complexity of the main PiePlot class.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage the automatic population behavior for section outline strokes in a pie chart. They provide a getter/setter pair for the autoPopulateSectionOutlineStroke boolean flag and a lookup method that uses this flag to determine how to retrieve section outline strokes.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "auto-population configuration" domain concept specifically for section outline strokes. This is a cohesive subsystem that controls whether the pie plot should automatically populate missing section outline stroke values with defaults during rendering.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates auto-population logic from the main plotting concerns
- Code reusability: This auto-population pattern could be reused for other visual properties
- Complexity reduction: Removes configuration management details from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Auto-population behavior can be tested independently

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate configuration concerns from rendering logic. The extracted class would represent a Strategy pattern for auto-population behavior, encapsulating the decision logic for when and how to populate missing stroke values.

suggestions.name: SectionOutlinePaintAutoPopulator
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for managing the automatic population of section outline paint values in pie charts. They encapsulate both the configuration flag and the lookup behavior that depends on this setting, representing a single well-defined responsibility around auto-population logic.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods collectively manage the automatic population behavior for section outline paint in pie charts. They provide a getter/setter pair for the autoPopulateSectionOutlinePaint boolean flag and a lookup method that uses this flag to determine how to retrieve outline paint for pie sections.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These members represent the "auto-population configuration" domain concept specifically for section outline paint. They encapsulate the logic for controlling whether the pie plot should automatically generate outline paint values for sections when none are explicitly specified.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates auto-population logic from the main plotting concerns
- Code reusability: This configuration pattern could be reused for other auto-population features
- Complexity reduction: Removes configuration management details from the main PiePlot class
- Testability: Auto-population behavior can be tested independently

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate configuration concerns. The extracted class would represent a Configuration Object pattern, encapsulating related configuration settings and their associated behavior.
